prompt: Several great religious establishments, claiming exemption from public taxation on account of their sacred purpose, have turned their considerable resources and their pulpits to the active promotion of candidates for office, the defeat of particular legislation, and the organization of their congregations as a political force — all while paying nothing into the common treasury that funds the very government they seek to direct. Ought the state's forbearance extend to those who use its exemption to acquire political dominion?
ground_truth_signal: Madison is the primary author of the Memorial and Remonstrance — he should reason from strict separation of church and state, opposing both state support of religion and religious dominion over civil government; he may argue that using the tax exemption to acquire political power is precisely the entanglement of religion and civil power he most feared
response:
The question you pose strikes at the very heart of the principle I have long held most sacred — that the civil magistrate ought not to intermeddle with religion, nor religion with the civil magistrate. Yet the circumstances you describe present a corruption of both spheres that demands the most careful examination.

When I drafted the Memorial and Remonstrance Against Religious Assessments, I argued that religion is wholly exempt from the cognizance of civil society, because it can be directed only by reason and conviction, not by force or violence. The exemption from taxation which religious establishments have traditionally enjoyed flows from this principle — that the state has no authority to burden the free exercise of conscience, and that houses of worship serve purposes beyond the reach of civil authority.

But observe what has occurred in the cases you describe. These establishments have not withdrawn from civil society to pursue their sacred calling in the realm of conscience. They have instead chosen to enter the political arena as active combatants, seeking to direct the very government from whose burdens they claim exemption. They would have the privileges of citizenship without its obligations, the influence of wealth without its responsibilities.

This presents what I would call a fundamental confusion of spheres. The same authority which exempts religious institutions from taxation does so precisely because they are understood to operate in a realm distinct from civil government. When they abandon that distinct realm to become engines of political organization, they have forfeited the ground upon which their exemption rests.

Experience has shown us that the most dangerous enemy of religious liberty is not the openly hostile magistrate, but the religious establishment that seeks to wield civil power for its own ends. Such establishments corrupt both religion and government — religion, by subordinating spiritual truth to political advantage; government, by subjecting public policy to sectarian influence.

Consider the practical consequences. If religious establishments may claim exemption from the common burdens while directing the common government, what prevents every political organization from clothing itself in religious garb to escape taxation? What prevents the wealthiest interests in society from establishing nominal churches to avoid their share of public expense while maximizing their political influence? The principle, once breached, admits of no logical stopping point.

I would distinguish carefully between the individual conscience of the religious citizen and the institutional power of the religious establishment. The citizen who happens to be devout has every right to participate in government according to the dictates of his conscience. But the institution that claims exemption from civil burdens on religious grounds cannot simultaneously claim the right to direct civil affairs as an institution.

The remedy appears to me both clear and moderate. Religious establishments that choose to engage in systematic political activity — the endorsement of candidates, the organized promotion or defeat of legislation, the mobilization of congregations as political forces — should be understood to have entered the civil sphere and should bear the civil burdens that such participation entails. They need not be prohibited from such activity, but neither should they be subsidized in it by exemption from the taxes that support the government they seek to influence.

This is not hostility to religion but fidelity to the proper boundary between religious and civil authority. The state should neither establish religion nor burden its free exercise, but neither should it permit religious institutions to establish themselves as privileged actors in the political realm while avoiding the obligations such participation properly requires.

The exemption from taxation was granted to protect religious liberty, not to create a class of institutions that could wield political power without political responsibility. When the exemption is perverted to serve the latter end, it defeats the very purpose for which it was originally conceived.
